name: Stack & Queue Synthesis — Calculator, LRU Cache, Task Scheduler

metadata.career_relevance: LRU Cache (LeetCode #146) is the most commonly asked design question at Razorpay/HDFC tech. It combines HashMap + doubly linked list. Basic Calculator tests expression evaluation mastery. These signal ability to combine data structures — the mark of a mid-senior engineer.

hook.what_you_will_build: LRU Cache (O(1) ops), Basic Calculator (stack eval), Decode String (stack).

visual_aid.description: LRU Cache with capacity 3. Initial state: head↔tail (empty). put(1): head↔[1]↔tail. put(2): head↔[2]↔[1]↔tail. put(3): head↔[3]↔[2]↔[1]↔tail. get(1): move 1 to front → head↔[1]↔[3]↔[2]↔tail. put(4): evict LRU (2, at tail.prev) → head↔[4]↔[1]↔[3]↔tail. Map updated at each step.
visual_aid.alt_text: LRU Cache with HashMap and doubly linked list

worked_example.problem_statement:
Evaluate expression string with +,-,*,/ and spaces. No parentheses.
'3+2*2'→7, '3/2'→1, '3+5/2'→5.

step_by_step_solution.details: Parse number, then apply previous operator to stack. + and -: push ±num. * and /: pop and apply immediately (higher precedence). Sum the stack at end.

things_to_notice: Previous operator applied to current number: op is the operator BEFORE current number
+ push num, - push -num, * pop and multiply, / pop and divide
Sum stack at end: handles all + case correctly

industry_examples.use_case: LRU merchant profile cache: O(1) get/put. DLL + HashMap combination.

applications.interview_relevance: LRU is the definitive cache design question. Basic Calculator series (I, II, III) tests operator precedence with stack. Decode String tests nested structure traversal.
applications.real_world_workflow: Stack for: expression eval, brackets, DFS. Queue for: BFS, FIFO scheduling. Deque for: sliding window, palindrome check, work-stealing. HashMap+DLL for: LRU, LFU cache.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Not knowing which node to evict from the DLL. LRU = Least Recently Used = the node closest to TAIL (tail.prev with our sentinel setup). Most recent = head.next.
common_gaps.remediation: Convention: head side = most recent, tail side = least recent. addFront (after head) = mark as most recent. Evict tail.prev = evict LRU. Always use sentinel head and tail — eliminates null checks for prev/next.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: LRU evicts wrong node — gets MRU instead of LRU

key_takeaways: LRU: HashMap (O(1) lookup) + DLL (O(1) reorder). Both needed for O(1) total.
DLL sentinels: head=most recent end, tail=least recent end
Basic Calculator: previous operator decides how to push current number
Decode String: stack saves count+StringBuilder on '[', pops on ']'
Task Scheduler: use count array + cooldown — no queue needed

self_check_questions.question: Why does LRU Cache need BOTH a HashMap AND a doubly linked list? Why not just one of them?
self_check_questions.answer: HashMap alone: O(1) lookup by key, but cannot track access order efficiently. To find/evict the LRU item, you'd need to scan all entries = O(n). Doubly linked list alone: O(1) add to front / remove from back (access order maintained), but lookup by key is O(n) linear scan. Combined: HashMap gives O(1) key → node lookup. DLL gives O(1) node reordering (move to front) and O(1) eviction (remove tail). The doubly-linked part is crucial: to remove a node, you need to update BOTH prev and next. Singly linked list would require O(n) to find prev. That's why Java's LinkedHashMap (which implements LRU) uses a doubly linked list internally.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Module 33 complete! Next: M34 DSA6 — Hash Tables (HashMap internals, collision resolution, custom hashCode, frequency patterns).
